text: NIHSS is not just a score — it's a roadmap. Systematically walk through it: consciousness, gaze, visual fields, facial palsy, arm/leg motor, limb ataxia, sensory, language, dysarthria, neglect. It tells you exactly where the lesion is.

Time is brain. Approximately 1.9 million neurons die every minute during an ischaemic stroke. The mantra 'treat fast' has genuine biological basis — the faster the reperfusion, the greater the salvage of penumbral tissue.

When asked about the future of stroke care, discuss the expansion of mechanical thrombectomy to 24-hour windows (DAWN, DEFUSE 3 trials) and the role of AI in automated perfusion imaging (RapidAI) to select patients beyond conventional time windows.

Secondary prevention is where neurology meets general medicine. Rate- vs rhythm-control in AF, dual antiplatelet in minor stroke/TIA (POINT trial), and statin intensity matter enormously — knowing this signals clinical depth.

Blood biomarkers in stroke are an active frontier: GFAP and UCH-L1 can distinguish haemorrhagic from ischaemic stroke within minutes. SERUM-biomarker-guided triage may one day reduce unnecessary CT head delays.

Audit idea: Door-to-needle times for thrombolysis at your trust vs national benchmark (60 min). Even small QIPs on checklist-driven protocols for ED stroke activation have strong publication potential.

Clinically isolated syndrome (CIS) — a first demyelinating episode — does not equal MS. MS diagnosis requires dissemination in time AND space (McDonald Criteria 2017). One T2 lesion with simultaneous gadolinium enhancement counts as both.

Uhthoff's phenomenon: transient neurological worsening with heat or exercise. This is NOT a relapse. Telling a patient this distinction avoids unnecessary MRI and steroid courses. It occurs because heat slows axonal conduction velocity in demyelinated fibres.
When assessing an MS patient, always ask about fatigue, bladder dysfunction, sexual dysfunction, and cognitive fog — these are often the most disabling symptoms and are frequently under-reported.

The MS treatment paradigm has shifted from 'escalation' to 'treat to target' — some centres now argue for early high-efficacy therapy (natalizumab, ocrelizumab, alemtuzumab) rather than starting with platform therapies, especially in young patients with high lesion burden.

Serum neurofilament light chain (sNfL) is emerging as a sensitive biomarker of neuroaxonal injury. Elevated sNfL correlates with relapse activity, disability progression, and response to DMTs — potentially enabling monitoring without annual MRI.

The cardinal features are TRAP: Tremor (rest, pill-rolling, 4-6Hz), Rigidity (cogwheel), Akinesia/bradykinesia, Postural instability. But bradykinesia is the sine qua non — you cannot diagnose PD without it.

Red flags for Parkinson's Plus syndromes: early falls (PSP), early autonomic failure (MSA), early dementia (DLB or MSA), symmetrical onset, poor levodopa response, cerebellar features. These should trigger rethinking the diagnosis.

Levodopa timing matters on the ward. Missed doses cause potentially life-threatening 'off' periods with immobility, dysphagia, and aspiration. Never stop Parkinson's medications perioperatively without specialist input.

Discuss disease-modification vs symptomatic treatment — currently all approved PD therapies are symptomatic. Alpha-synuclein-targeted therapies (e.g. Prasinezumab) are in Phase 2/3 trials and represent the frontier of PD research.

GBA1 mutations are the most common genetic risk factor for Parkinson's disease, conferring a 5–10× lifetime risk. This has opened the door to genotype-stratified trials and potential lysosomal pathway therapies.

Not all seizures are epilepsy. ILAE definition requires either 2+ unprovoked seizures ≥24h apart, OR one seizure with ≥60% recurrence risk. A provoked seizure (hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia, alcohol withdrawal) does not = epilepsy.

PNES (Psychogenic Non-Epileptic Seizures) affects ~20% of patients referred to epilepsy services and is frequently misdiagnosed and over-treated. Features suggesting PNES: eyes closed during event, prolonged duration >2 min, ictal crying, preserved awareness during bilateral motor activity.

Status epilepticus management: the 30-minute rule. Lorazepam → repeat lorazepam → IV levetiracetam or sodium valproate → anaesthesia. Know this sequence and know the doses. On call neurologists are called for status — having this fluent impresses.

The epilepsy surgical revolution: SEEG (stereoelectroencephalography), robotic-assisted placement, and increasingly precise 3T MRI/PET co-registration mean more patients with drug-resistant epilepsy are candidates for curative surgery. This is a compelling area of growth in UK neurology.

SCN1A mutations (Dravet syndrome) are the prototypic genetic epilepsy. Sodium channel blockers (carbamazepine, lamotrigine) are contraindicated and worsen seizures — a critical clinical-genomics crossover that matters on the wards and in interviews.

Alzheimer's ≠ just memory loss. Posterior cortical atrophy causes visuospatial failure; logopenic aphasia variant affects language; frontal variant presents as behavioural change. Knowing the variants makes you sound far beyond the average IMT doctor.

Reversible causes of cognitive decline: B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, NPH (wet/wobbly/wacky triad), subdural haematoma, HIV, syphilis, Wernicke's. These should be excluded before any dementia diagnosis is accepted.

DLB vs Alzheimer's: fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, and parkinsonism are the core DLB features. Critical point: these patients are exquisitely sensitive to antipsychotics — can cause severe, irreversible NMS-like reactions. Never prescribe haloperidol in DLB.

The Lecanemab and Donanemab approval era is transforming Alzheimer's care. These anti-amyloid monoclonal antibodies are the first DMTs shown to slow cognitive decline in early AD. Key concerns: ARIA (amyloid-related imaging abnormalities), patient selection (early disease, APOE4 genotype), and NHS accessibility.

Blood-based biomarkers for AD: plasma p-tau217, p-tau181, and Abeta42/40 ratio can now detect AD pathology with diagnostic accuracy approaching CSF. This is set to transform screening, clinical trial recruitment, and potentially diagnostic pathways in 5–10 years.

Thunderclap headache = subarachnoid haemorrhage until proven otherwise. Non-contrast CT head within 6h has ~98% sensitivity. If CT negative and suspicion remains: LP at 12h post-onset looking for xanthochromia.

Medication overuse headache (MOH) affects ~1–2% of the population and is the most underrecognised cause of chronic daily headache. Simple analgesics, triptans, or opioids used >10–15 days/month for 3+ months. Withdrawal is the treatment — warn patients headache initially worsens.

CGRP monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab) are a genuine paradigm shift in migraine prevention — the first migraine-specific preventatives. Knowing the mechanism (blocking CGRP or its receptor at the trigeminal level) signals research-level understanding.

Relative Afferent Pupillary Defect (RAPD) — the swinging torch test — is one of the most important signs in neurology. RAPD = optic nerve or severe retinal disease. No RAPD in a 'blind' eye suggests non-organic visual loss.

CN III palsy algorithm: complete ptosis + down-and-out eye + dilated pupil = posterior communicating artery aneurysm until proven otherwise. This is a neurosurgical emergency. Pupil-sparing CN III palsy is more likely microvascular (diabetes/hypertension).

Foot drop is peroneal nerve until proven otherwise — but always exclude L4/L5 radiculopathy (hip abductor weakness absent in peroneal palsy, present in radiculopathy) and sciatic nerve lesion (hamstrings affected).

When asked 'why neurology?': frame it around diagnostic reasoning, the growing therapeutic landscape (the nihilism era is over), and the intersection of basic science and clinical medicine. Avoid generic answers — reference specific cases, podcasts, or experiences.

The cerebellar speech triad: dysarthria (slurred), dysmetria (past pointing), dysdiadochokinesia (impaired rapid alternating movements). DANISH — Dysdiadochokinesia, Ataxia, Nystagmus, Intention tremor, Slurred speech, Hypotonia.

EDS ≠ fatigue. True excessive daytime sleepiness (unintended lapses into sleep) is rare in chronic insomnia — patients usually describe fatigue, poor concentration or mood symptoms instead. Misreading fatigue as sleepiness leads to unnecessary sleep-disorder workups.
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: Pitfall: not all insomnia is insomnia. ~30% of OSA presents primarily as insomnia, and RLS/PLMD are frequently mislabelled as insomnia — especially in patients on antidepressants or antihistaminergic drugs. COMISA (comorbid insomnia + sleep apnoea) affects ~40% of insomnia patients — sleep comorbidity is the norm, not the exception.
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: Red flags that argue against a simple insomnia diagnosis and favour further sleep testing: true EDS, bed-partner-reported OSA/RLS/PLMD features, no preceding 'sleep reactivity', no sleep-initiation difficulty, and isolated maintenance issues with multiple brief awakenings rather than prolonged wake periods.
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: Pitfall: overlooking circadian rhythm sleep disorders (CRSD). ~22% of CRSD is misdiagnosed as insomnia. Key clue: when unburdened by work/school demands (weekends, holidays), CRSD patients sleep a normal duration and feel refreshed — true insomniacs don't. Delayed sleep phase disorder is the most common CRSD, especially in adolescents (prevalence 3.3–4%).
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: Melatonin timing is everything. For phase-advancing a delayed sleep phase disorder, maximal effect comes from a low dose (0.5mg) taken ~10 hours before the calculated mean sleep midpoint (roughly 4 hours before average sleep onset) — not a high hypnotic dose at bedtime, which can shift the clock the wrong way.
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: CBTi is first-line, not medication. Cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia is non-inferior to drugs, with 60–80% response rates and 50–60% remission at 6–12 months. Avoid standard CBTi's sleep-restriction component in migraine, epilepsy and CFS/ME, as it can exacerbate these conditions.
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: Daridorexant, a dual orexin receptor antagonist, is NICE-approved (2024) for insomnia disorder after failed/unsuitable CBTi. Low dependence risk, no rebound insomnia, minimal respiratory suppression (safe in COMISA) — but watch for vivid dreams, sleep paralysis and monitor mood in depression. ~70% response rate, works better for maintenance than initiation.
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: Mirtazapine dosing pitfall: sedating effect is maximal around 15mg — higher doses can paradoxically become more alerting due to increasing noradrenergic/serotonergic activity outweighing antihistaminergic sedation. Amitriptyline, though commonly prescribed, is less evidence-based than mirtazapine and its sleep benefit is more short-lived.
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: Hypersomnolence terminology matters. EDS = inability to stay awake during major waking episodes. 'Excessive need for sleep' = subjective report not resolved by more sleep. Hypersomnia = objective finding of ≥10h sleep per 24h. 'Pure' neurological hypersomnolence is rare — central disorders (except NT1) are diagnoses of exclusion after ruling out drugs, OSA, PLMD, CRSD and psychiatric causes.
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: NT1 vs NT2 vs idiopathic hypersomnia: NT1 = EDS + cataplexy (or MSLT mean latency ≤8min with ≥2 SOREMPs, or low CSF orexin <110pg/mL). NT2 = EDS >3 months, no cataplexy, same MSLT criteria, normal/unmeasured CSF orexin. Idiopathic hypersomnia = EDS >3 months, no cataplexy, ≤1 SOREMP, plus either MSLT latency ≤8min or total sleep time >11h over 24h monitoring.
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: Pitfall: over-relying on the MSLT. Without a preceding polysomnography night to confirm ≥6h sleep, the MSLT is invalid. Even with strict thresholds (<5min latency), up to 30% of 'positive' results don't have narcolepsy — sleep deprivation and CRSD are common false-positive drivers. MSLT is highly reliable for NT1 (93–100% sensitivity) but much less reliable for NT2 and idiopathic hypersomnia, and has poor test-retest reliability.
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: CSF orexin and HLA testing both have real limits. ~5% of typical cataplexy patients have normal CSF orexin. HLA DQB1*06:02 is present in 95–99% of NT1 — but also in up to 25% of White Europeans and 38% of Africans without narcolepsy, so a positive result doesn't confirm NT1 and a negative one doesn't exclude NT2.
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: Narcolepsy therapeutics are moving fast. Beyond modafinil/stimulants and anticataplectics (clomipramine, venlafaxine, sodium oxybate), newer agents include pitolisant (H3 inverse agonist, 50–60% response, no dependence) and solriamfetol (NDRI, also helps residual OSA sleepiness but doesn't treat cataplexy). The frontier is OX2 receptor agonists — TAK-994 showed dramatic wakefulness gains but was halted for hepatotoxicity; oveporexton (phase 3) and alixorexton (phase 2) are the current leads.
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: NREM parasomnia vs REM sleep behaviour disorder (RBD): NREM events (sleepwalking, confusional arousal, sleep-eating) occur in the first third of the night, are infrequent per night, and recall is partial/absent. RBD occurs later in the night, involves violent dream-enactment with full dream recall on waking, and is a red flag for underlying α-synucleinopathy (Parkinson's, DLB) — even a 'typical' RBD history still needs polysomnography to confirm and rule out mimics.
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: Sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy is an important parasomnia mimic: brief (<2min) stereotyped events (kicking, cycling, tonic posturing) recurring multiple times a night with abrupt cessation, often early in the night. Up to 30% arise from extrafrontal (temporal/insular) foci, not just frontal lobe as classically taught.
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: When to scan for parasomnias: polysomnography adds little when the history is classically NREM, but is warranted for atypical presentations, suspected RBD, sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy, functional/dissociative events, adult-onset events (NREM parasomnias usually start in childhood), violent/injurious events, or features suggesting OSA/RLS/PLMD (present in ~30% of NREM parasomnia patients and can trigger events).
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: Zolpidem is specifically implicated in triggering NREM parasomnias. First-line RBD treatment is clonazepam or melatonin — clonazepam is more effective, but melatonin is better tolerated (less sleepiness, unsteadiness, cognitive impairment).
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: Pitfall: dopamine agonists in RLS cause augmentation. Ropinirole, pramipexole and rotigotine are no longer AASM first-line for RLS — chronic use causes augmentation (worsening, earlier-onset, spreading symptoms) in 30–70% of patients by 10 years, plus impulse-control disorders. If used, keep doses low (e.g. ropinirole ≤2mg, pramipexole ≤0.5mg).
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: RLS work-up should always include ferritin. Target serum ferritin >75–100µg/L with transferrin saturation >20–45%, supplementing iron as needed. Current AASM first-line pharmacotherapy for chronic RLS is α2δ ligands (gabapentin, pregabalin) — effective and augmentation-free, especially useful with comorbid anxiety, chronic pain or insomnia.
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

text: Managing established augmentation: taper dopamine agonists gradually over weeks (never abruptly), replete iron stores first, and bridge with gabapentinoids or low-dose opioids (buprenorphine, prolonged-release oxycodone-naloxone) through the transition, then keep patients on non-dopaminergic therapy long-term.
source: Adenan 2026, Pract Neurol — Sleep neurology: pearls and pitfalls

title: Multiple Sclerosis: ABN 2024 Disease-Modifying Therapy Strategy
source: ABN Acute Neuro Bootcamp 2025

clinicalPearls.title: Think beyond RRMS/SPMS/PPMS
clinicalPearls.pearl: Traditional MS subtypes are less useful for treatment decisions. Modern practice focuses on the degree of ongoing inflammatory disease activity rather than simply the disease label.

clinicalPearls.title: Treat early
clinicalPearls.pearl: Disease-modifying therapy should be started as early as possible after diagnosis of active MS to reduce irreversible neurological injury.

clinicalPearls.title: High-efficacy therapy is increasingly first-line
clinicalPearls.pearl: ABN 2024 recommends considering high-efficacy DMT as an initial treatment option in eligible patients rather than automatically using an escalation approach.

clinicalPearls.title: Evidence favours early inflammatory suppression
clinicalPearls.pearl: Observational studies suggest starting high-efficacy therapy within the first two years of disease onset is associated with improved long-term disability outcomes, although definitive RCTs are still awaited.

clinicalPearls.title: Shared decision-making is essential
clinicalPearls.pearl: Choice of DMT should balance efficacy, safety, pregnancy plans, comorbidities, infection risk, monitoring burden and patient preferences.

clinicalPearls.title: Poor prognostic features favour early intensive therapy
clinicalPearls.pearl: Frequent relapses, high MRI lesion burden, spinal cord lesions, pyramidal tract involvement and early disability predict worse long-term outcomes.

highYieldFacts.fact: Early inflammatory damage contributes disproportionately to future disability.
Escalation therapy is no longer the default strategy for all newly diagnosed patients.
Treatment decisions should be individualised and discussed within the MS MDT.
Disease activity on MRI may precede clinical deterioration.

clinicalDecisionFramework.question: New diagnosis of active MS
approach: Consider high-efficacy DMT first if appropriate

examPearls: ABN guidance has shifted away from routine escalation therapy.
High-efficacy DMT should be considered early in patients with active disease.
Treatment decisions should not rely solely on RRMS/SPMS/PPMS classification.
MRI activity is an important marker of ongoing disease even without clinical relapse.
takeHomeMessage: Modern MS management aims to suppress inflammatory disease early. Eligible patients should be offered discussion of high-efficacy therapy soon after diagnosis, with treatment individualised through shared decision-making.
